critic_raw:
## Analysis of GNN-PPI Methodology

### Issue Group 1: Graph Leakage Through the Interaction Graph Itself

**Claim:** GNN-PPI uses the protein interaction graph to propagate relational information, improving generalization to novel proteins.

**Why this might be wrong:** This is the central methodological tension. The GNN operates on the protein interaction graph — but that graph *is* the training data. When BFS/DFS splitting is used to ensure some proteins appear only in test, those novel proteins have no edges in the training graph by construction. The GNN cannot pass messages to or from nodes it has never seen. So for the inter-novel-protein interaction setting the paper specifically targets, the GNN degrades to a form of sequence-only encoding anyway (whatever the node featurization is), because the message-passing phase has no edges to traverse for these nodes.

If the authors handle this by including test-set proteins in the graph but excluding their edge labels from the training loss (a transductive setting), then information about the *existence* of edges between novel proteins has leaked into the graph structure. The model has seen which novel proteins are connected, even if not the interaction type. This is a form of structural leakage: the graph topology itself encodes the test interactions.

If they handle it inductively (novel proteins truly absent from the graph during training), then the GNN's advantage over sequence-only baselines like PIPR cannot come from graph message-passing for the inter-novel-protein subset — it can only come from differences in node featurization or pooling. The attribution of gains to "leveraging correlations through the interaction graph" would then be misleading for the primary evaluation target.

**Evidence:** The paper should report what graph is presented to the GNN at inference time for novel proteins, and ablate: (a) GNN with only known-protein edges vs. (b) GNN with all edges. If performance on inter-novel-protein interactions doesn't drop when novel-protein edges are removed from the inference graph, the gains are not coming from graph structure for that subset.

---

### Issue Group 2: Micro-F1 Conflates Interaction Type Difficulty

**Claim:** Micro-F1 is used as the main metric to assess multi-type PPI prediction quality.

**Why this might be wrong:** Micro-F1 weights each sample equally, meaning it is dominated by the most frequent interaction types. PPI datasets are typically heavily imbalanced across interaction types (binding interactions, for example, vastly outnumber regulatory ones). A model that gets the dominant types right while performing poorly on rare types will report high micro-F1. Given the paper's stated emphasis on inter-novel-protein interactions — which may preferentially involve certain interaction types (e.g., newly discovered interactions may be less common types) — micro-F1 could systematically mask exactly the failure modes the paper claims to address.

Furthermore, if the novel proteins in the BFS/DFS test splits tend to be peripheral nodes in the graph (by construction of BFS/DFS — they are nodes added last), they may be associated with sparser or rarer interaction types, making the micro-F1 metric doubly misleading.

**Evidence:** Report per-class F1 or macro-F1 alongside micro-F1, specifically broken down for the inter-novel-protein subset. If micro-F1 and macro-F1 diverge substantially on that subset, the metric is hiding something.

---

### Issue Group 3: BFS/DFS Splits Create Structurally Biased Test Sets

**Claim:** BFS and DFS graph-based splits provide a more realistic and consistent evaluation than random splits for novel protein generalization.

**Why this might be wrong:** BFS starting from a seed node will tend to isolate a topologically cohesive cluster as the test set — nodes at roughly the same graph distance from the seed. DFS will isolate a path-like chain. These are not random samples from the distribution of "all novel proteins one might encounter"; they are structurally specific subgraphs. In real-world deployment, novel proteins are not discovered in BFS or DFS order from any particular seed — they appear due to experimental priorities, organism coverage, disease relevance, etc.

This means the BFS/DFS splits may be evaluating generalization to a specific structural regime (peripheral or path-following nodes in the interaction graph) rather than generalization to novel proteins in general. A method that does well on BFS/DFS test sets might fail on novel proteins that are structurally central (e.g., hub proteins newly characterized in a new organism).

Moreover, the claim that BFS/DFS gives "more consistent" evaluation needs scrutiny: consistency across what? If two BFS runs with different seeds give very different results, the evaluation is neither consistent nor reliable.

**Evidence:** Report variance across multiple BFS/DFS seeds. Compare whether the ranking of methods is stable across seeds and across BFS vs. DFS. If method rankings change substantially across seeds, the evaluation framework is not robust. Also compare the degree distribution and structural properties of BFS/DFS test nodes vs. random-split test nodes to characterize what kind of novel proteins are actually being evaluated.

---

### Issue Group 4: Prerequisite Assumption — Node Features Must Carry Enough Signal for Inductive Generalization

**Claim:** GNN-PPI generalizes to novel proteins by leveraging graph structure.

**Why this is a prerequisite, not just a performance question:** For the GNN mechanism to operate at all on novel proteins, the node featurization must place novel proteins in a meaningful position in the feature space relative to training proteins. If node features are sequence-derived embeddings (e.g., from ESM or simple amino acid composition), the quality of those embeddings determines whether any graph-based method can generalize — the graph structure just propagates those features. If two proteins share a sequence-level feature representation but have different interaction profiles, no amount of graph message-passing on training data corrects this for test nodes.

The paper's framing attributes gains to graph relational structure, but the prerequisite is that sequence features are good enough. If they are, a non-graph method with the same node features might do equally well on novel proteins. If they aren't, the graph cannot rescue the model.

**Evidence:** Ablate the node featurization: (a) same GNN architecture with random node features, (b) GNN with sequence features, (c) sequence-only model (no GNN) with the same sequence features used as GNN node features. If (c) ≈ (b) on the inter-novel-protein subset, the graph is not contributing to the claimed gains for that setting.

---

### Summary Assessment

The most serious issue is Issue Group 1: there is a fundamental ambiguity about whether the GNN is operating transductively (with structural leakage) or inductively (in which case graph message-passing cannot explain the gains on the targeted setting). This is not a minor concern — it potentially invalidates the core methodological claim. Issue Group 3 is also substantive: BFS/DFS splits may create structurally atypical test sets that do not reflect the distribution of novel proteins in practice, undermining the "more realistic evaluation" claim. Issues 2 and 4 are real but secondary.
all_issues_raised: GNN message-passing cannot reach truly novel proteins inductively, so gains on inter-novel-protein interactions either stem from structural leakage (transductive setting) or cannot be attributed to graph relational modeling at all.
Micro-F1 is dominated by frequent interaction types and may mask poor performance on rare types that are disproportionately represented among novel-protein interactions.

BFS/DFS splits produce structurally atypical test sets (peripheral or path-following nodes) that do not represent the distribution of novel proteins encountered in practice, and consistency across seeds is not demonstrated.

The GNN's ability to generalize to novel proteins is predicated on the quality of sequence-based node features, but no ablation isolates whether gains come from graph structure versus node featurization, making the mechanistic claim unverified.
all_issues_adjudicated: GNN message-passing cannot reach truly novel proteins inductively, so gains on inter-novel-protein interactions either stem from structural leakage (transductive setting) or cannot be attributed to graph relational modeling at all.
Micro-F1 is dominated by frequent interaction types and may mask poor performance on rare types that are disproportionately represented among novel-protein interactions.

BFS/DFS splits produce structurally atypical test sets (peripheral or path-following nodes) that do not represent the distribution of novel proteins encountered in practice, and consistency across seeds is not demonstrated.

The GNN's ability to generalize to novel proteins is predicated on the quality of sequence-based node features, but no ablation isolates whether gains come from graph structure versus node featurization, making the mechanistic claim unverified.
